NeuroMed University Hospital NHS Trust Discharge Summary
Patient: P001 | NHS: 432 890 1234
Neurosurgery Ward / Neuro-Oncology
PATIENT INFORMATION
Patient Name: James R. Thornton
Date of Birth: 14 March 1962
Age: 63 years
Sex: M
NHS Number: 432 890 1234
Blood Group: A+
Height / Weight: 183.2 cm / 63.4 kg
BSA: 1.61 m²
Ethnicity: White British
Referring GP: Dr. Sarah Patel, The Grange Practice, Leeds
DIAGNOSIS
Histology: Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM)
WHO Grade: IV
ICD-O3 Code: 9440/3
Primary Location: right parietal
Laterality: right
Surgery Date: 07 November 2023
Surgeon: Mr. Jonathan Hale (Consultant Neurosurgeon)
Extent of Resection: Gross Total Resection (GTR) — >98% EOR
MOLECULAR PROFILE
Idh Status: Wild-type
Mgmt Promoter Methylation: Unmethylated
Egfr Amplification: True
Codeletion 1P19Q: False
Tert Promoter Mutation: Mutant C250T
Atrx Status: Lost
Cdkn2A B Homozygous Deletion: False
Pten Loss: True
Ki67 Proliferation Index Percent: 66.1
CLINICAL PRESENTATION
Symptom Duration: 15 weeks
Presenting Symptoms: papilloedema, Cushing's triad, headache (worse in morning)
KPS at Triage: 90
ECOG at Triage: 1

Urgency: URGENT (score 3/5)
TREATMENT & CLINICAL COURSE
Patient James R. Thornton was diagnosed with Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM) WHO Grade IV on 2023-10-21. Following
Gross Total Resection (GTR) — >98% EOR, the patient commenced concurrent chemoradiotherapy with Temozolomide
(TMZ). Over 4 imaging time-points, the overall radiological trajectory demonstrates True Progression.
MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE
Drug Name Dose Route Schedule
Temozolomide (TMZ) 75 mg/m² PO Daily concurrent with RT
Temozolomide (TMZ) adjuvant 150 mg/m² PO Days 1-5 of 28-day cycle
Stupp protocol RT mg/m²
Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) mg/m²
Valproate sodium mg/m² PO
Dexamethasone mg/m²
Omeprazole mg/m²
Fluconazole mg/m²
GP HANDOVER NOTE
Dear Dr. Sarah Patel,
Re: James R. Thornton, DOB: 1962-03-14, NHS: 432 890 1234
Thank you for continuing to support this patient in the community. James was diagnosed with Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM)
WHO Grade IV (MGMT Unmethylated, IDH Wild-type) on 2023-10-21. They underwent Gross Total Resection (GTR) —
>98% EOR followed by Temozolomide (TMZ).
Current medications: Temozolomide (TMZ), Temozolomide (TMZ) adjuvant, Stupp protocol RT, Low-molecular-weight
heparin (LMWH) — please review for community prescribing.
Current performance status: KPS 90 / ECOG 1.
URGENT: Please contact neuro-oncology if the patient presents with new neurological symptoms, seizure, or sudden
deterioration.
Yours sincerely,
Neuro-Oncology MDT
FOLLOW-UP PLAN
Recommended Action: Urgent MDT review within 72 hours
Triage Alerts: Steroid dose may need escalation — current 4mg dexamethasone,
Consider re-biopsy or liquid biopsy if progression confirmed
Next Review: As per oncology MDT schedule
Urgent Contact: On-call Neuro-Oncology: 0800 123 4567
Discharge summary prepared by NeuroMed University Hospital NHS Trust Neuro-Oncology Team. Please file in patient records. For clinical
queries: neurooncology@neuromed.nhs.uk

NeuroMed University Hospital NHS Trust Histopathology Report
Lab Ref: PATH-P001-2023 | NHS: 432
Department of Neuropathology
890 1234
PATIENT & SPECIMEN DETAILS
Patient Name: James R. Thornton
Date of Birth: 14 March 1962
NHS Number: 432 890 1234
Sex: M
Specimen Type: Brain tumour biopsy / resection specimen
Surgery Date: 07 November 2023
Surgeon: Mr. Jonathan Hale (Consultant Neurosurgeon)
Specimen Received: 07 November 2023
Reporting Pathologist: Dr. R. Patel, FRCPath — Consultant Neuropathologist
Authorised: 07 November 2023
MACROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
Multiple tissue fragments received in formalin, aggregating to approximately 2.1 x 1.8 x 0.9 cm. Tissue is pale grey-white with
focal areas of haemorrhage. Surgical margins not assessable on submitted fragments.
HISTOLOGICAL REPORT
NEUROPATHOLOGY REPORT nnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn
nnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn Patient: James R. Thornton DOB: 1962-03-14 Hospital ID: P001 Specimen type: Brain
biopsy / resection specimen Date of procedure: 2023-09-26 Reported by: Dr. Idris Okoye, FRCPath — Consultant
Neuropathologist nnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnnn
nnnnnnnnnnnn
CLINICAL INFORMATION: 63-year-old male presenting with seizures and progressive neurological deficits. MRI
demonstrated a ring-enhancing mass lesion within the left frontal lobe.
MACROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION: Received in formalin: Multiple fragments of soft, grey-tan haemorrhagic tissue, aggregate
2.8 × 1.5 × 0.9 cm. Tissue submitted entirely for processing. Blocks: A1–A4.
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION: Sections show a highly cellular infiltrating glial neoplasm demonstrating marked nuclear
pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses/10 HPF), microvascular proliferation (endothelial multi-layering, glomeruloid
vascular tufts), and geographic necrosis with pseudopalisading. Perineuronal satellitosis and subpial spread are identified.
The surrounding neuropil shows reactive astrogliosis.
IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY: GFAP: Strong diffuse positive IDH1 R132H: Negative ATRX: Loss of nuclear expression p53:
Wild-type pattern Ki-67: Approximately 38.1% positivity in hotspot areas EGFR: Overexpressed (3+) PTEN: Loss of
expression
MOLECULAR PROFILE: MGMT promoter methylation (pyrosequencing): Unmethylated IDH1/2 sequencing (next-generation
sequencing panel): Wild-type TERT promoter mutation: C250T detected CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion (FISH): Not
detected EGFR amplification (FISH): Detected, copy number 23 PTEN deletion: Heterozygous deletion detected
DIAGNOSIS: GLIOBLASTOMA MULTIFORME (GBM), WHO CNS Grade IV ICD-O-3: 9440/3 WHO 2021 CNS Tumour
Classification criteria met.
SYNOPSIS: The morphological, immunohistochemical, and molecular findings confirm a WHO Grade IV IDH wild-type
Glioblastoma. The unmethylated MGMT promoter status is an adverse prognostic marker and predicts relative resistance to
temozolomide chemotherapy. Recommend Stupp protocol discussion at neuro-oncology MDT.
Signed electronically: Dr. Idris Okoye, FRCPath — Consultant Neuropathologist Date of report: 2023-10-08

MOLECULAR / IHC SUMMARY
Idh Status: Wild-type
Mgmt Promoter Methylation: Unmethylated
Egfr Amplification: True
Codeletion 1P19Q: False
Tert Promoter Mutation: Mutant C250T
Atrx Status: Lost
Cdkn2A B Homozygous Deletion: False
Pten Loss: True
Ki67 Proliferation Index Percent: 66.1
FINAL DIAGNOSIS
Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM) — WHO Grade IV
ICD-O3: 9440/3 | Location: right parietal | Laterality: right
This report is issued by the NeuroMed University Hospital NHS Trust Neuropathology Department. Diagnostic queries:
neuropathology@neuromed.nhs.uk | Ext. 6210. Classification per WHO CNS5 2021.

findings.description: Large, heterogeneous mass in the right parietal lobe, consistent with Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM). The mass exhibits significant surrounding edema.

v1.impression: Enhancing mass in the right parietal lobe, consistent with Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM). Significant surrounding edema and mass effect are present.

The image shows a large, enhancing, heterogeneous mass in the right parietal lobe, which is consistent with the diagnosis stated in the report header (GBM, right parietal), but the specific findings and measurements are not documented in the body of the report.

results.title: NRG Oncology/RTOG1205: A Randomized Phase II Trial of Concurrent Bevacizumab and Reirradiation Versus Bevacizumab Alone as Treatment for Recurrent Glioblastoma.
results.abstract: PURPOSE: To assess whether reirradiation (re-RT) and concurrent bevacizumab (BEV) improve overall survival (OS) and/or progression-free survival (PFS), compared with BEV alone in recurrent glioblastoma (GBM). The primary objective was OS, and secondary objectives included PFS, response rate, and treatment adverse events (AEs) including delayed CNS toxicities. METHODS: NRG Oncology/RTOG1205 is a prospective, phase II, randomized trial of re-RT and BEV versus BEV alone. Stratification factors included age, resection, and Karnofsky performance status (KPS). Patients with recurrent GBM with ima...
results.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

results.title: Bevacizumab Alone and in Combination With Irinotecan in Recurrent Glioblastoma.
results.abstract: PURPOSE: We evaluated the efficacy of bevacizumab, alone and in combination with irinotecan, in patients with recurrent glioblastoma in a phase II, multicenter, open-label, noncomparative trial. PATIENTS AND METHODS: One hundred sixty-seven patients were randomly assigned to receive bevacizumab 10 mg/kg alone or in combination with irinotecan 340 mg/m RESULTS: In the bevacizumab-alone and the bevacizumab-plus-irinotecan groups, estimated 6-month progression-free survival rates were 42.6% and 50.3%, respectively; objective response rates were 28.2% and 37.8%, respectively; and median overall...
results.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology

results.title: Glioblastoma Treatment: State-of-the-Art and Future Perspectives.
results.abstract: (1) Background: Glioblastoma is the most frequent and lethal primary tumor of the central nervous system. Through many years, research has brought various advances in glioblastoma treatment. At this time, glioblastoma management is based on maximal safe surgical resection, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy with temozolomide. Recently, bevacizumab has been added to the treatment arsenal for the recurrent scenario. Nevertheless, patients with glioblastoma still have a poor prognosis. Therefore, many efforts are being made in different clinical research areas to find a new alternative to improve...

results.title: Reirradiation in recurrent glioblastoma.
results.abstract: PURPOSE OF REVIEW: Reirradiation has emerged as a potentially valuable treatment strategy for recurrent glioblastoma, a disease characterized by inevitable local progression despite aggressive multimodal first-line therapy. Recent advances in radiotherapy techniques, improved patient selection, and evolving systemic treatment combinations have renewed clinical interest in this approach. This is reflected by recent publication of the first international consensus guidelines (ESTRO/EANO) and the initiation of an European phase III randomized trial on reirradiation of patients with recurrent g...

results.title: Treatment options for progression or recurrence of glioblastoma: a network meta-analysis.
results.abstract: BACKGROUND: Glioblastoma (GBM) is a highly malignant brain tumour that almost inevitably progresses or recurs after first line standard of care. There is no consensus regarding the best treatment/s to offer people upon disease progression or recurrence. For the purposes of this review, progression and recurrence are considered as one entity. OBJECTIVES: To evaluate the effectiveness of further treatment/s for first and subsequent progression or recurrence of glioblastoma (GBM) among people who have received the standard of care (Stupp protocol) for primary treatment of the disease; and to p...
results.journal: The Cochrane database of systematic reviews

treatment_proposal.reason: Neurosurgeon's urgent concern about midline shift requiring decompression takes precedence over systemic therapy, while neuro-oncologist and pharmacist highlight guideline non-concordance and toxicity risks. Neuroradiologist's uncertainty about pseudoprogression adds imaging urgency.
modifications: Defer systemic therapy until post-surgical imaging confirms true progression
Refer to NCT06558214 trial (if ECOG improves) or consider bevacizumab monotherapy per NCCN guidelines
Implement PJP prophylaxis if bevacizumab resumes
treatment_proposal.clinical_narrative: The patient requires urgent neurosurgical evaluation for decompression due to critical midline shift, which cannot safely await systemic therapy. Bevacizumab must be paused pre-operatively due to wound-healing risks. Post-surgery, imaging must confirm true progression before resuming therapy. Given ECOG 2 status and toxicity concerns, bevacizumab monotherapy (NCCN-recommended) or trial referral (if ECOG improves) should be prioritized over high-toxicity combos.

debate_transcript.statement: The RANO criteria classification of PD relies on RECIST data, but critical imaging details (T2/FLAIR changes, enhancing lesion characteristics, perfusion/MRS data) are absent to distinguish pseudoprogression from true progression. The new lesion flag in RECIST may represent treatment effect rather than tumor growth, requiring advanced imaging confirmation.
concerns: Inability to differentiate pseudoprogression from true progression without advanced imaging (perfusion/MRS/PET)
RECIST new lesion flag may overestimate tumor burden in the context of bevacizumab treatment

debate_transcript.statement: The patient's critical midline shift necessitates urgent surgical decompression to prevent herniation, which cannot safely be delayed for systemic therapy. Bevacizumab's use concurrent with planned surgery is contraindicated due to its wound-healing impairment.
concerns: Midline shift requiring immediate decompressive intervention
Bevacizumab use concurrent with planned surgery

debate_transcript.statement: The proposed regimens (bevacizumab + entrectinib/carmustine/pembrolizumab) are not aligned with current NCCN/EANO guidelines for recurrent glioblastoma, which prioritize reirradiation + bevacizumab (PMID:36260832) or trials with novel agents (e.g., NCT06810544). The patient’s ECOG 2 (severely limited activity) precludes most systemic therapies, and the lack of MGMT/IDH biomarker data limits precision in selecting salvage options.
concerns: ECOG 2 performance status makes most systemic therapies (including proposed regimens) too toxic per NCCN guidelines
No MGMT promoter methylation or IDH mutation data to guide bevacizumab efficacy or alternative therapies
Clinical trial NCT06810544 requires ECOG 0-1, which this patient does not meet

debate_transcript.statement: The proposed regimens (bevacizumab + entrectinib/carmustine/pembrolizumab) carry significant toxicity risks. FAERS data shows bevacizumab alone has 3,354 reports of death (14.6% serious) and frequent grade 3/4 diarrhea/nausea. Given the patient's ECOG 2 performance status, prior dose reduction, and cumulative lomustine exposure, these combos may be too toxic. Consider substituting carmustine with a less myelosuppressive agent.
concerns: FDA-FAERS: 3,354 reports of death (14.6% serious) with bevacizumab combinations
Bevacizumab + carmustine may exacerbate myelosuppression (patient has prior dose reduction)
ECOG 2 performance status may limit tolerance of novel combos with high-grade toxicity

debate_transcript.statement: The critical midline shift and new enhancing lesion on RECIST PD classification require urgent differentiation between pseudoprogression (common post-bevacizumab) and true progression. RANO criteria should be applied, as glioblastoma response assessment depends on T2/FLAIR changes and contrast-enhancing lesion dynamics, not RECIST. Current imaging lacks perfusion/MRS data to confirm tumor viability vs. treatment effect.
concerns: Inadequate imaging to distinguish pseudoprogression from true progression
RECIST criteria misapplied for glioblastoma; RANO assessment required

debate_transcript.statement: The critical midline shift indicates urgent need for decompressive surgery to prevent herniation, but bevacizumab use contraindicates elective surgery due to impaired wound healing. Surgical resection/biopsy should precede systemic therapy to address mass effect and confirm tumor biology, though ECOG 2 status limits surgical tolerance.
concerns: Midline shift requires urgent decompression before initiating or continuing bevacizumab-based therapy
Bevacizumab use precludes safe surgical intervention due to wound healing complications

debate_transcript.statement: The proposed regimens (bevacizumab + entrectinib/carmustine/pembrolizumab) lack NCCN/EANO guideline support for recurrent glioblastoma. Bevacizumab monotherapy remains a standard second-line option (PMID:37935104), but combinations with entrectinib or pembrolizumab are not endorsed. The patient’s ECOG 2 status and prior dose reduction raise significant concerns about tolerability of these high-toxicity regimens (PMID:34559423). Clinical trial referral (e.g., NCT06558214) should be prioritized given the absence of biomarker data (MGMT/IDH) and limited evidence for proposed combos.
concerns: Proposed regimens not aligned with NCCN/EANO guidelines for recurrent GBM
ECOG 2 performance status may limit tolerance of high-toxicity combinations
Lack of molecular biomarker data (MGMT/IDH) to guide therapy
FAERS data highlights bevacizumab combo toxicity risks (3,354 death reports, 14.6% serious)

debate_transcript.statement: The proposed regimens (bevacizumab + entrectinib/carmustine/pembrolizumab) pose significant toxicity risks given the patient's ECOG 2 performance status, prior dose reductions, and FAERS signals of severe adverse events (e.g., 3,354 reports of death with bevacizumab alone). Bevacizumab's hypertension risk synergizes with carmustine, and myelosuppression from lomustine + carmustine/pembrolizumab could worsen platelet/ANC deficits. Dose reductions or substitutions are urgently needed.
concerns: ECOG 2 performance status incompatible with high-toxicity regimens (FAERS: 3,354 death reports with bevacizumab alone)
Bevacizumab + carmustine combo risks additive myelosuppression and nephrotoxicity (FAERS: 4,348 diarrhea, 3,580 nausea reports)
Lack of MGMT/IDH biomarker data to justify bevacizumab use in recurrent GBM
No PJP prophylaxis planned despite bevacizumab's immunosuppressive effects

debate_transcript.statement: Urgent surgical decompression required pre-therapy; systemic regimen deferred pending post-op imaging and ECOG reassessment.
concerns: Midline shift requiring immediate decompression precludes safe bevacizumab use
ECOG 2 status limits tolerance of high-toxicity regimens (FAERS: 3,354 death reports with bevacizumab combos)

interactions.mechanism: Steroid-induced hypertension may compound bevacizumab hypertensive effect
interactions.recommendation: Monitor BP; antihypertensive therapy may be required

interactions.mechanism: No known pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interactions between bevacizumab (a VEGF inhibitor) and fluconazole (a CYP450 inhibitor). Bevacizumab is not metabolized by CYP enzymes, and fluconazole does not significantly alter bevacizumab's clearance. Both drugs are generally well-tolerated together in clinical practice.
interactions.recommendation: No dose adjustment or additional monitoring is required for this combination. Continue both medications as prescribed, and monitor for standard adverse effects of each drug (e.g., hypertension, nephrotoxicity for bevacizumab; gastrointestinal effects for fluconazole).

interactions.mechanism: Both bevacizumab and lomustine may contribute to myelosuppression, potentially increasing the risk of hematologic toxicity (e.g., neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) when used concurrently. There is no direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction, but additive effects on bone marrow function are possible.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor complete blood counts frequently during combination therapy. Consider dose adjustments or delays based on hematologic parameters. No definitive contraindication exists, but caution is advised due to overlapping myelosuppressive risks.

interactions.mechanism: Bevacizumab increases the risk of thromboembolic events due to anti-angiogenic effects, while LMWH is an anticoagulant. Concurrent use may paradoxically increase bleeding risk (due to LMWH's anticoagulant effect) or thrombosis risk (due to bevacizumab's pro-thrombotic effects), depending on the balance between anticoagulation and vascular integrity.
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use if possible. If required, closely monitor for signs of bleeding or thrombosis, and adjust LMWH dosing based on clinical response and coagulation parameters. Consult hematology/oncology for risk-benefit evaluation.

interactions.mechanism: Omeprazole may increase the risk of gastrointestinal perforation when used with bevacizumab, though the evidence is not definitive. This could be related to bevacizumab's anti-angiogenic effects and potential interactions with gastric mucosal integrity.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of gastrointestinal perforation (e.g., abdominal pain, perforation symptoms). Consider alternative acid suppression strategies (e.g., H2 receptor antagonists) if clinically appropriate, but no absolute contraindication exists.

interactions.mechanism: Standard combination in recurrent GBM; no adverse PK interaction
interactions.recommendation: Standard of care combination

interactions.mechanism: Bevacizumab may inhibit the metabolism of valproate sodium, leading to increased plasma concentrations of valproate and a heightened risk of valproate toxicity (e.g., encephalopathy, hepatotoxicity).
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use if possible. If co-administration is necessary, closely monitor valproate levels and consider dose reduction of valproate. Consult a specialist for risk-benefit evaluation.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, which are involved in the metabolism of dexamethasone, leading to increased dexamethasone plasma concentrations.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of dexamethasone toxicity (e.g., hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, osteoporosis). Consider dose adjustment of dexamethasone if clinically indicated. Use alternative antifungal agents if feasible, but ensure close monitoring if fluconazole is required.

interactions.mechanism: Dexamethasone may induce hepatic enzymes (e.g., CYP3A4), potentially altering lomustine metabolism. However, lomustine is primarily metabolized via non-CYP pathways, and clinical evidence of significant interaction is limited.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for lomustine efficacy (e.g., tumor response) and dexamethasone-related adverse effects (e.g., immunosuppression). No dose adjustment is typically required, but consider close follow-up in patients with suboptimal lomustine response.

interactions.mechanism: Pharmacodynamic interaction: Dexamethasone may increase the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding, which could be additive to the bleeding risk associated with low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH). However, no direct metabolic or pharmacokinetic interaction is established between the two drugs.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of bleeding (e.g., gastrointestinal symptoms, bruising) and adjust LMWH dose if clinically indicated. No dose adjustment of dexamethasone or LMWH is required based on current evidence, but caution is advised in patients with a history of bleeding or thrombocytopenia.

interactions.mechanism: Omeprazole may inhibit CYP3A4, a metabolic pathway for dexamethasone, potentially increasing dexamethasone plasma concentrations. However, the clinical significance of this interaction is limited due to the weak inhibitory effect of omeprazole on CYP3A4 and the wide therapeutic index of dexamethasone.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for increased corticosteroid effects (e.g., hyperglycemia, fluid retention). No dose adjustment is typically required, but consider alternative PPIs (e.g., pantoprazole) if clinically appropriate to minimize interaction risk.

interactions.mechanism: Corticosteroids may reduce temozolomide oral bioavailability
interactions.recommendation: Separate administration by at least 1 hour

interactions.mechanism: Dexamethasone may increase the hepatic metabolism of valproate sodium via induction of CYP enzymes (e.g., CYP2C9), potentially reducing valproate plasma concentrations and efficacy.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor valproate levels closely and consider dose adjustment if subtherapeutic levels are detected. Avoid abrupt discontinuation of either drug. Use alternative corticosteroids if feasible, but prioritize clinical judgment based on patient-specific factors.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole is a CYP3A4 inhibitor, but lomustine is primarily metabolized via non-CYP pathways (e.g., hydrolysis). However, both drugs may contribute to myelosuppression, increasing the risk of hematologic toxicity.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor blood counts closely and consider dose adjustment of lomustine if significant myelosuppression occurs. No routine dose adjustment of fluconazole is required.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole may reduce renal clearance of low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) by impairing renal function, potentially increasing LMWH plasma concentrations and bleeding risk. However, this interaction is not well-established and depends on individual renal function.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor renal function and LMWH anti-Xa levels closely. Adjust LMWH dose if renal impairment develops. Consider alternative antifungal agents if significant renal dysfunction is present or if bleeding risk is high.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4, which is involved in the metabolism of omeprazole, potentially increasing omeprazole plasma concentrations.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for omeprazole-related adverse effects (e.g., hypomagnesemia, gastrointestinal symptoms). Consider alternative proton pump inhibitors (e.g., pantoprazole) if clinically feasible, as they have less CYP3A4 dependence.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, the primary metabolic enzyme for temozolomide, leading to increased plasma concentrations of temozolomide and heightened risk of toxicity.
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use of fluconazole and temozolomide. If antifungal therapy is required, consider alternative agents with minimal CYP2C9 inhibition (e.g., itraconazole, posaconazole) and monitor temozolomide levels if co-administration is unavoidable.

interactions.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits hepatic metabolism of valproate sodium, potentially increasing valproate plasma concentrations and risk of toxicity (e.g., hepatotoxicity, encephalopathy).
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use if possible. If required, closely monitor valproate levels and consider dose reduction of valproate sodium. Alternative antifungal agents (e.g., itraconazole) may be considered if feasible.

interactions.mechanism: Lomustine may cause myelosuppression, including thrombocytopenia, which could increase the risk of bleeding when combined with anticoagulation from low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH). However, there is no direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between the two drugs.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of bleeding and platelet counts. Adjust LMWH dose if thrombocytopenia is severe or if bleeding risk is elevated. No absolute contraindication, but caution is advised.

interactions.mechanism: No significant drug-drug interaction mechanism identified between lomustine and omeprazole. Lomustine is not a substrate for CYP2C19 or CYP3A4, which are inhibited by omeprazole, and no evidence suggests omeprazole affects lomustine absorption or metabolism.
interactions.recommendation: No dose adjustment or monitoring is required for concurrent use of lomustine and omeprazole in neuro-oncology patients.

interactions.mechanism: Additive myelosuppression; both agents are alkylating
interactions.recommendation: Requires dose reduction and close haematologic monitoring

interactions.mechanism: Lomustine inhibits the hepatic metabolism of valproate sodium, leading to increased plasma concentrations of valproate and a heightened risk of valproate toxicity, including fatal hepatotoxicity.
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use of lomustine and valproate sodium. If co-administration is unavoidable, closely monitor valproate levels and consider dose reduction of valproate, but this combination is generally contraindicated due to the significant risk of severe toxicity.

interactions.mechanism: Omeprazole may increase the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding when used with anticoagulants like LMWH, though there is no direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between the two drugs. The increased bleeding risk is due to the combined effects of anticoagulation and potential gastric mucosal protection from omeprazole, which may mask early signs of bleeding.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor the patient closely for signs of bleeding. Consider alternative proton pump inhibitors (e.g., pantoprazole) if clinically feasible, as some evidence suggests they may have a lower risk of interacting with anticoagulants. If omeprazole is necessary, ensure appropriate bleeding precautions are in place.

interactions.mechanism: Additive risk of bleeding due to the anticoagulant effect of low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) and the myelosuppressive effects of temozolomide, which may reduce platelet counts and increase hemorrhagic risk.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of bleeding (e.g., bruising, hematuria, gastrointestinal bleeding) and assess platelet counts regularly. Adjust LMWH dose based on renal function and platelet levels if indicated. Consider alternative anticoagulation strategies if bleeding risk is high.

interactions.mechanism: Valproate sodium may cause thrombocytopenia, which, when combined with the anticoagulant effects of low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH), could increase the risk of bleeding due to additive effects on hemostasis.
interactions.recommendation: Monitor for signs of bleeding (e.g., bruising, hematuria, gastrointestinal bleeding). Consider adjusting LMWH dose or valproate if bleeding occurs, and ensure coagulation parameters (e.g., platelet count, INR) are closely followed.

interactions.mechanism: No clinically significant interaction
interactions.recommendation: May be used for GI protection

interactions.mechanism: Omeprazole may inhibit hepatic metabolism of valproate sodium, potentially increasing valproate plasma concentrations via CYP2C19 inhibition and/or reduced renal clearance. This could increase risk of valproate toxicity (e.g., encephalopathy, hepatotoxicity).
interactions.recommendation: Monitor valproate levels closely; consider dose reduction of valproate or alternative PPI (e.g., H2 receptor antagonist) if feasible. Avoid concurrent use in patients with renal impairment or those at higher risk for valproate toxicity.

interactions.mechanism: Valproate sodium inhibits carboxylesterase 1 (CES1), an enzyme involved in the metabolic activation of temozolomide, leading to increased plasma concentrations of temozolomide and potential toxicity.
interactions.recommendation: Avoid concomitant use if possible. If co-administration is necessary, closely monitor for temozolomide-related toxicity (e.g., myelosuppression, neurotoxicity) and consider dose reduction of temozolomide. Alternative anticonvulsants (e.g., levetiracetam) may be preferred.

MRI v2: Enhancement of the right parietal lobe lesion, consistent with Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM). The lesion shows signs of
MDT Board Discussion Required — escalated for multidisciplinary review
Survival Prediction: PFS=13.0w (CI 1.6–114.9w), RECIST delta predicted=+5.7%, σ=15.343

timeline.narrative: Patient diagnosed with glioblastoma. Latest RECIST response: PD. Currently on 7 medication(s): Bevacizumab, Lomustine, Dexamethasone, Omeprazole, Fluconazole.

summary.patient_letter:
Dear Patient,

We are writing to update you about your recent scans and treatment. MRI results show that the tumour in your right parietal lobe has grown since your last scan, and a new area of concern has been found. This suggests the condition is progressing. Due to the tumour's size and location, there is pressure on parts of your brain, which requires urgent attention. Your care team is discussing next steps, including possible surgery to relieve this pressure. Currently, you are receiving medications including Bevacizumab, Lomustine, and Dexamethasone to manage symptoms and slow progression. We are also monitoring for potential drug interactions, which your pharmacist will review with you. Your treatment plan may need adjustments based on upcoming tests. Please ensure you attend all appointments and report any new symptoms promptly. This is a research prototype. Please discuss any questions with your care team.
summary.gp_handover_letter: Diagnosis: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) with progressive disease (PD) per RECIST 1.1. Imaging: Right parietal lobe enhancing lesion with increased perilesional edema and mass effect; new lesion detected. RECIST: PD confirmed by new lesion (automatic classification). Medications: Bevacizumab, Lomustine, Dexamethasone, Omeprazole, Fluconazole, Valproate sodium. Interactions: Major interactions include bevacizumab+valproate sodium, bevacizumab+LMWH, fluconazole+valproate sodium; contraindications include lomustine+valproate sodium, temozolomide+valproate sodium, fluconazole+temozolomide. Plan: Urgent neurosurgical evaluation required for decompression due to critical midline shift. Defer systemic therapy until post-surgical imaging confirms true progression. Consider bevacizumab monotherapy (NCCN) or trial referral (NCT06558214) if ECOG improves. Initiate PJP prophylaxis if bevacizumab resumes. Monitor for toxicity; MDT discussion required for treatment optimization. Survival prediction: PFS median 13 weeks (CI 1.6-114.9w).

qa_pairs.question: What is the current assessment of the tumor's progression based on the MRI findings?
qa_pairs.answer: The MRI report indicates progressive disease (PD) with enlargement of the enhancing lesion, new satellite foci, and increased perilesional oedema, consistent with glioblastoma multiforme (GBM).

qa_pairs.question: What is the significance of the RANO criteria response being classified as PD?
qa_pairs.answer: The RANO criteria response of PD, along with worsened neurologic status, suggests disease progression despite treatment. This may necessitate a modification of the current regimen.

qa_pairs.question: What is the rationale for modifying the current treatment regimen?
qa_pairs.answer: The treatment proposal suggests modifying the regimen due to progressive disease (PD) and the need for further optimization, potentially involving clinical trials such as NCT06810544.

qa_pairs.question: Are there any new lesions detected, and how does this affect the treatment plan?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, a new lesion was detected, and this, combined with the PD classification, indicates a need for urgent intervention. The treatment plan may involve deferring current therapies and exploring alternative options.
